network.overall_strategy: This network is built to reflect the current literature reality that prebiotic arginine synthesis is unresolved. Accordingly, the graph centers the best-supported constructive branch: cyanosulfidic surface chemistry leading to an arginine alpha-aminonitrile precursor, followed by hydrolysis to arginine. Additional pathways are included as literature-grounded or explicitly speculative variants that converge on the same limited set of supported intermediates, especially cyanamide, ornithine, citrulline, beta-aminopropionaldehyde, and the arginine aminonitrile precursor. Hydrothermal chemistry supplies simple reduced carbon feedstocks and nitrogen-bearing intermediates that feed surface photochemistry, while surface minerals and UV drive the key guanidinium-installing chemistry. Biochemical/prebiotic steps emphasize preservation, concentration, and downstream incorporation rather than claiming solved arginine biogenesis.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate
reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface reduction of CO2 by H2 on iron-sulfur phases yields C1 reduced carbon as formate.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrothermal C1 fixation on Fe-S minerals is widely used as the reduced-carbon entry point for vent chemistry and is consistent with alkaline vent models linking greigite-like catalysts to CO2 reduction; this network uses it only as feedstock provisioning rather than a direct arginine-forming claim (Huber & Wachtershauser, 1997; Jordan et al., 2021; Kitadai, 2015).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal reductive carbon fixation to pyruvate
reactions.mechanism: Stepwise mineral-mediated CO2 reduction and C-C bond formation generate alpha-keto acid products represented here by pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate is included because it appears in the only trace abiotic arginine report and is a central hydrothermal metabolic hub, although its direct coupling to arginine remains unresolved; this step is therefore a feedstock-enabling hydrothermal node rather than a claimed validated arginine pathway (Kitadai, 2015; Amend & Shock, 1998; Fujioka et al., 2009).

reactions.name: Surface photochemical formation of cyanamide from reduced nitrogen-carbon feedstocks

conditions.catalyst: TiO2 under UV with phosphate-buffered shallow pools
conditions.other: strong UV, anaerobic, photochemical approximation of reduced-atmosphere cyanamide generation

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical oxidation-rearrangement within reduced C/N pools furnishes cyanamide as a reactive guanylating agent.
reactions.reasoning: Cyanamide is explicitly required by the literature as the key guanylating agent. Its prebiotic formation is supported by UV irradiation of reduced gas mixtures and by its central role in cyanosulfidic protometabolism, but a fully resolved mineral-specific surface pool mechanism remains incomplete; this node is therefore a constrained connector grounded in reported prebiotic availability rather than a single definitive pathway (Schimpl, Lemmon & Calvin, 1965; Patel et al., 2015; Powner, Gerland & Sutherland, 2009).

reactions.name: Surface UV production of guanidine from ammonium cyanide chemistry

conditions.other: pH 8.4-8.5, 254 nm UV, anaerobic, days to weeks

reactions.mechanism: UV-driven ammonium cyanide condensation produces guanidine as a stable nitrogen-rich small molecule.
reactions.reasoning: Lohrmann reported guanidine in 8-20% yield from irradiated ammonium cyanide with iron catalyst. Because guanidine is prebiotically accessible and stable, it is included as a side-node relevant to guanidinium chemistry, though not as a demonstrated direct precursor-to-arginine route (Lohrmann, 1972; Robertson & Miller, 1995).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic cross-coupling to acrylonitrile

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox system in phosphate buffer on sulfide-rich mineral surfaces

reactions.mechanism: Cu-mediated photoredox cross-coupling of HCN with acetylene under cyanosulfidic conditions generates acrylonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This is the first experimentally detailed step in the Patel-Sutherland arginine precursor route, performed under 35C anaerobic cyanosulfidic photochemistry with H2S and copper photoredox chemistry; mineral sulfides and phosphate are included as required environmental agents (Patel et al., 2015; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland, 2017).

reactions.name: Amination of acrylonitrile to beta-aminopropionitrile

conditions.catalyst: cyanosulfidic aqueous medium with phosphate buffering

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of ammonia to activated nitrile-alkene gives beta-aminopropionitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This step follows directly from the Patel/Sutherland route to the arginine precursor network, where acrylonitrile reacts onward to aminonitrile products under the same cyanosulfidic setting (Patel et al., 2015; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland, 2017).

reactions.name: Photoreductive conversion of beta-aminopropionitrile to beta-aminopropionaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: Cu photoredox and UV-active mineral surfaces

reactions.mechanism: Photoredox reduction transforms the nitrile-derived intermediate into the corresponding aldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: The literature explicitly places beta-aminopropionaldehyde in the cyanosulfidic sequence preceding cyanamide guanylation. Exact isolated yields were not reported for the arginine branch, but the sequence is directly described (Patel et al., 2015; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland, 2017).

reactions.name: Guanylation of beta-aminopropionaldehyde by cyanamide

conditions.catalyst: cyanamide in aqueous phosphate-buffered evaporitic pool chemistry
conditions.other: 35C, anaerobic; described as especially efficient in cyanosulfidic network

reactions.mechanism: Primary amine attack on cyanamide installs a guanidino functionality onto the aminated aldehyde backbone.
reactions.reasoning: The especially efficient reaction of beta-aminopropionaldehyde with cyanamide is the decisive literature-supported guanidinium-installing step that motivates the arginine branch of the cyanosulfidic network (Patel et al., 2015; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland, 2017).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic extension to arginine alpha-aminonitrile precursor

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox system with H2S and phosphate buffer
conditions.other: 35C, anaerobic, UV irradiation, further photoreduction plus HCN addition

reactions.mechanism: Photoreduction and HCN incorporation furnish the alpha-aminonitrile precursor of arginine (compound 46).
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. explicitly propose that compound 46, the alpha-aminonitrile precursor of arginine, would be formed alongside the proline-precursor branch. Tsanakopoulou and Sutherland later confirmed the route logic, though quantitative arginine-branch yields remain unreported (Patel et al., 2015; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland, 2017).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of arginine aminonitrile precursor to arginine

conditions.other: mild aqueous hydrolysis in anoxic pools; avoids destructive high-temperature hydrothermal conditions

reactions.mechanism: Aminonitrile hydrolysis converts the nitrile terminus to the alpha-amino acid carboxylate, yielding arginine.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of alpha-aminonitriles to amino acids is standard prebiotic logic, and Patel et al. specifically frame compound 46 as the aminonitrile precursor of arginine. Because arginine is thermally labile, the hydrolysis is assigned to mild post-surface aqueous conditions rather than hot vents (Patel et al., 2015; Miller & Bada, 1988; Korner et al., 2021).

reactions.name: Direct guanylation of ornithine by cyanamide to arginine

conditions.catalyst: alkaline aqueous/drying interface; prebiotic mineral concentration environment
conditions.other: conceptual prebiotic analog of aqueous guanylation; literature supports chemistry but not continuous primitive demonstration

reactions.mechanism: The delta-amino group of ornithine attacks cyanamide, directly generating the guanidinium side chain of arginine.
reactions.reasoning: This is the classic ornithine-plus-cyanamide route first demonstrated by Schulze and Winterstein. Modern aqueous guanylation by cyanamide can be highly efficient, but the best catalytic yields use non-prebiotic catalysts, so the present node is literature-grounded yet still incomplete as a fully demonstrated prebiotic sequence. Montmorillonite is included as a plausible concentrating/protective mineral rather than a proven catalytic equivalent (Schulze & Winterstein, 1899; Tsubokura et al., 2014; Johnson et al., 2008; Catalano et al., 2024).

reactions.name: HCN oligomer hydrolysis products include urea and cyanate pool

conditions.other: anoxic shallow pool chemistry; hydrolysis of HCN-derived oligomers

reactions.mechanism: HCN polymerization followed by hydrolysis releases simple carbamoyl-related products including urea and cyanate-bearing species.
reactions.reasoning: The literature supports HCN oligomer hydrolysates as sources of urea and related N-C-O species, and cyanate is discussed as a plausible prebiotic carbamoyl donor more stable than carbamoyl phosphate. This node is deliberately conservative and avoids overclaiming direct stoichiometric citrulline synthesis from HCN alone (Ferris et al., 1978; Juarez et al., 2021).

reactions.name: Carbamoylation of ornithine by cyanate to citrulline

conditions.other: hypothetical prebiotic urea-cycle analog under mild conditions

reactions.mechanism: The terminal amino group of ornithine is carbamoylated by cyanate to furnish citrulline.
reactions.reasoning: This reaction is explicitly a hypothetical prebiotic analog motivated by the literature: citrulline has been reported among HCN oligomer hydrolysates, and cyanate is proposed as a plausible prebiotic carbamoyl donor. It is included as a speculative branch and not as a validated route to arginine (Ferris et al., 1978; Juarez et al., 2021).

reactions.name: Speculative conversion of citrulline plus cyanamide toward arginine

conditions.other: speculative mild pool chemistry; no direct experimental demonstration

reactions.mechanism: Hypothetical guanyl-transfer/deoxygenative rearrangement from a carbamoylated ornithine analog toward the guanidinium amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: The deep research report identifies the prebiotic urea-cycle analog as hypothetical and notes no experimental demonstration of citrulline-to-arginine conversion. This node is therefore explicitly speculative and included only to represent that proposed literature branch without overstating it (Ferris et al., 1978; Juarez et al., 2021; Cunin et al., 1986).

reactions.name: Trace arginine formation from pyruvic acid and hydroxylamine under supercritical CO2/H2O

conditions.catalyst: supercritical CO2/H2O environment with mineral surfaces
conditions.other: 60C, acid hydrolysis workup in reported experiment; trace yield only

reactions.mechanism: Unresolved multistep condensation/reduction chemistry under supercritical CO2/H2O gave trace arginine after hydrolytic analysis.
reactions.reasoning: Fujioka et al. reported only 0.32 micromol arginine from hydroxylamine hydrochloride and pyruvic acid, with no elucidated mechanism and far higher alanine formation. This node is retained solely as a literature-reported trace side-path, not a strong productive route, and it is kept under mild hydrothermal conditions consistent with the paper rather than hot black-smoker chemistry known to destroy arginine (Fujioka et al., 2009; Miller & Bada, 1988; Korner et al., 2021).

reactions.name: Montmorillonite-mediated adsorption and preservation of arginine
reactions.mechanism: Arginine partitions into montmorillonite interlayers by cation exchange, increasing concentration and protection from degradation.
reactions.reasoning: Although represented as a state-preserving self-node for network continuity, the chemistry is literature-based: montmorillonite strongly and selectively adsorbs arginine, which would help protect and concentrate it after rare formation events. This is not a synthesis step but a preservation/concentration process important to prebiotic plausibility (Catalano et al., 2024).

reactions.name: Wet-dry peptide bond formation from arginine to arginyl-arginine

conditions.other: wet-dry cycling on clay/mineral interfaces; concentrated salts

reactions.mechanism: Repeated dehydration-rehydration promotes condensation of arginine into short peptides after concentration on mineral surfaces.
reactions.reasoning: This downstream biochemical step reflects literature that deliquescent minerals and clay-associated systems can support amino-acid oligomerization, while arginine-clay interlayers can undergo peptide formation under dry heating. It is included as a utilization branch, not as evidence for arginine synthesis itself (Campbell et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2019; Huber & Wachtershauser, 1998).

pathways.name: Canonical cyanosulfidic arginine precursor route
pathways.description: Best-supported constructive route from HCN/acetylene photochemistry through beta-aminopropionaldehyde, cyanamide guanylation, and arginine aminonitrile hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic route with independent cyanamide provisioning
pathways.description: Same Patel-Sutherland branch but emphasizing cyanamide as a separately formed surface hub that converges with the aldehyde branch.

pathways.name: Direct ornithine cyanamide guanylation
pathways.description: Classical conceptual route in which prebiotically available ornithine and cyanamide meet in surface pools to form arginine directly.

pathways.name: Direct ornithine guanylation followed by clay capture
pathways.description: Direct ornithine-to-arginine route coupled to montmorillonite concentration and protection after formation.

pathways.name: Direct ornithine guanylation followed by peptide incorporation
pathways.description: Arginine formed by ornithine guanylation is then incorporated into a short peptide under wet-dry prebiotic chemistry.

pathways.name: HCN-derived cyanate to citrulline speculative branch
pathways.description: Hypothetical urea-cycle analog in which HCN hydrolysis products supply cyanate that carbamoylates ornithine to citrulline, followed by speculative conversion to arginine.

pathways.name: Cyanamide-assisted citrulline branch with independent cyanamide source
pathways.description: Same hypothetical citrulline route, but explicitly requiring separately generated cyanamide to attempt final guanidinium installation.

pathways.name: Trace supercritical CO2/H2O arginine route
pathways.description: Literature-reported trace arginine formation from pyruvic acid and hydroxylamine under supercritical CO2/H2O conditions.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal feedstock to surface cyanosulfidic to arginine
pathways.description: Connected environmental route in which hydrothermal reduced carbon provisioning supports broader surface cyanide chemistry that yields the arginine aminonitrile branch.

pathways.name: Surface cyanosulfidic arginine formation with downstream preservation and incorporation
pathways.description: Arginine formed through the aminonitrile route is protected by clay adsorption and can participate in later peptide chemistry.
